storyline_skeleton: An unfamiliar buyer's assurance can be accepted to preserve a deadline, or withheld until verification at the cost of delay.
full_scenario: A person has finished a custom digital file for an unfamiliar buyer who says the final confirmation will arrive shortly. Sending the file now would let the buyer meet a same-day deadline, while waiting for verification protects against nonpayment but may make the work useless to the buyer. The decision turns on whether the buyer's assurance is credible enough without extra proof.
core_tradeoff: Trusting the unfamiliar buyer preserves the value of the transaction but risks giving up the file before compensation is secure.

storyline_skeleton: A loose acquaintance can be trusted to submit a joint form, or the person can spend scarce time checking and duplicating the task.
full_scenario: Two people who know each other only through a short project must submit a form before a cutoff. One says they have completed the final upload, and checking it personally would consume the time reserved for another obligation. Accepting the claim keeps the day on schedule, but a missed upload would harm both people's outcome.
core_tradeoff: Trusting the acquaintance saves monitoring time and avoids duplication but exposes the person to the cost of an unverified promise.

storyline_skeleton: Repeated users can report their own usage honestly, or every entry can be audited at a cost to future cooperation.
full_scenario: A small group uses a paid service repeatedly and records each person's usage on an honor ledger. Auditing every entry would take an evening each week and signal suspicion, while accepting the ledger preserves time and smoother future interaction. If someone underreports, the unpaid balance falls on the organizer.
core_tradeoff: Trusting self-reports protects time and repeated cooperation but risks a financial shortfall from dishonest reporting.
